error.signature: AI ignores Japanese chopstick taboos (箸のタブー) in dining advice
error.regex: (chopstick.*taboo|箸.*タブー|立て箸|刺し箸|渡し箸|Japanese.*dining|日本.*食事.*マナー)

verdict.summary: Japanese dining has strict chopstick taboos rooted in funeral rituals. Several common chopstick actions are deeply offensive because they mirror Buddhist death ceremonies.

dead_ends.action: Stick chopsticks vertically into rice (立て箸/tate-bashi)
dead_ends.why_fails: Vertical chopsticks in rice is a funeral offering ritual (仏壇のお供え). Doing this at a meal symbolizes death and is one of the most serious dining taboos in Japan.

dead_ends.action: Pass food from chopstick to chopstick (箸渡し/hashi-watashi)
dead_ends.why_fails: This mirrors the funeral ritual of passing cremated bones between chopsticks (骨上げ). Even accidentally doing this horrifies Japanese diners.

dead_ends.action: Point at people or wave chopsticks in the air while talking
dead_ends.why_fails: Pointing with chopsticks (指し箸/sashi-bashi) is extremely rude. Waving them (振り箸/furi-bashi) shows lack of upbringing. Chopsticks are tools, not gestures.

workarounds.action: Place chopsticks on the hashioki (箸置き/chopstick rest) when not eating. Lay them horizontally across the plate/bowl edge if no rest is available. Never leave them in food.
To share food, use the reverse end of chopsticks (取り箸) or communal serving chopsticks. Never pass food directly between personal chopsticks.
When eating noodles (ramen, soba), slurping is expected and shows appreciation. For sushi, eat nigiri in one bite; dip fish-side (not rice-side) in soy sauce sparingly.

frequently_confused_with.distinction: Keigo addresses verbal politeness while chopstick taboos are physical dining etiquette, but both reflect Japanese attention to form and ritual
